templates.title: Foundation
templates.subtitle: Morning activation — done before 8am
templates.coaching_note: This is the habit. Not a workout — a ritual. 20 minutes, every morning, before the day gets in the way. On rest days, on travel days, on tired days. The bar is low by design.

exercises.name: Skip Rope
exercises.form_cue: Light on your feet. Land on the balls of your feet, not flat. If no rope, do jumping jacks or high knees instead.
exercises.why: Gets the heart rate up and body temperature rising fast. No better way to shake off morning grogginess.

phases.name: Activation
phases.coaching_note: These aren't hard. They're setting you up for the day and protecting the hips and back.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge
exercises.form_cue: Drive through heels. Squeeze glutes at top and hold 1 second.
exercises.why: Wakes up the posterior chain before you load it during the day. Non-negotiable for lower back health.

exercises.name: Clamshell (pilates band)
exercises.form_cue: Lying on side, band just above knees, hips stacked. Rotate top knee up like a clamshell. Don't let the hip roll back.
exercises.why: Gluteus medius activation — directly targets the muscle group around the left iliac crest injury. Also protects knees on runs.

exercises.name: World's Greatest Stretch
exercises.form_cue: Lunge forward, drop back knee. Place same-side hand on floor. Reach opposite hand to sky, rotating torso. Hold 2 seconds at the top.
exercises.why: One movement that hits hip flexors, thoracic rotation, hamstrings, and glutes. Best single exercise for morning mobility.

exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.form_cue: Lower back pressed into floor. Extend opposite arm and leg slowly. Return slowly. Don't rush.
exercises.why: Deep core activation. Sets the spine for the day.

exercises.name: Calf Raise
exercises.form_cue: On the edge of a step or flat floor. Rise all the way up, lower all the way down. Don't bounce.
exercises.why: Calf and Achilles tendon conditioning. As run volume builds, this is injury prevention on every morning.

templates.progression_notes: On days when you have a strength or run session later, keep this light — it's activation, not fatigue. On rest days, you can extend the stretching section instead of doing the skip rope.

templates.title: Recovery
templates.subtitle: Foam rolling + mobility
templates.coaching_note: This is training. Not optional, not a reward — a session. The left hip and right elbow need this as much as the strength work does. Slow down, breathe into tight spots.

phases.name: Foam Rolling
phases.coaching_note: Roll slowly. When you find a tender spot, stop and hold 5-10 seconds. Don't just roll fast — that does nothing.

exercises.name: Quad Roll
exercises.form_cue: Face down, roller under quads. Use forearms to control pressure. Rotate leg inward and outward to hit different angles.
exercises.why: Tight quads pull the pelvis forward and load the lower back. Your run training will make this worse without maintenance.

exercises.name: IT Band / Outer Hip Roll
exercises.form_cue: Side-lying, roller on outer thigh. Stack top foot on floor for pressure control. Roll from hip to just above knee.
exercises.why: IT band tightness contributes to knee pain on runs. Your right-side injury dominance makes the right IT band a priority.

exercises.name: Glute / Piriformis Roll
exercises.form_cue: Sit on roller, cross ankle over opposite knee. Lean into the crossed-leg side. This targets the piriformis directly.
exercises.why: Left glute and piriformis are likely contributing to the iliac crest pain. This is your most important spot today.

exercises.name: Upper Back / Thoracic Roll
exercises.form_cue: Roller across upper back (T4-T8 range, not neck or lower back). Arms crossed over chest or hands behind head. Extend over the roller gently. Work segment by segment.
exercises.why: Thoracic restriction forces lumbar compensation — directly linked to the overhead badminton back injury pattern. Open this up.

exercises.name: Calf Roll
exercises.form_cue: Roller under calf. Stack legs for more pressure. Rotate foot inward and outward. Flex and point the ankle.
exercises.why: Tight calves reduce ankle dorsiflexion which loads the knee and hip on runs. Post-run essential.

phases.name: Stretching
phases.coaching_note: Hold each stretch at least 45-60 seconds. Don't bounce. Breathe into the stretch — exhale to go deeper.

exercises.name: Hip Flexor Stretch (kneeling lunge)
exercises.form_cue: Back knee on floor, front foot flat. Drive hips forward gently. Keep torso upright — don't lean forward.
exercises.why: Tight hip flexors tilt the pelvis and compress the lower back. Critical for running posture.

exercises.name: Pigeon Pose (QL / glute stretch)
exercises.form_cue: Front shin across mat, back leg extended. Fold forward over the front leg. If hips don't reach the floor, put a folded towel under the hip.
exercises.why: Directly targets the left glute and QL — the exact area around your iliac crest flag. Spend extra time on the left side.

exercises.name: Supine Hamstring Stretch
exercises.form_cue: Lying on back. Loop pilates band around foot. Straighten leg toward ceiling. Keep lower back flat on floor. Don't yank.
exercises.why: Tight hamstrings limit hip flexion in running stride and load the lower back.

exercises.name: Thoracic Rotation
exercises.form_cue: Lying on side, knees stacked at 90 degrees. Top arm sweeps across to the other side, following with the eye and head. Let the chest open. Hold 2 seconds at the end.
exercises.why: Directly addresses the thoracic mobility restriction linked to the badminton overhead back injury pattern.

exercises.name: Child's Pose
exercises.form_cue: Knees wide, arms extended forward, forehead to floor. Breathe into the lower back. Let gravity do the work.
exercises.why: Decompresses the lumbar spine. Good closer for any session where the lower back worked hard.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Stretch
exercises.form_cue: Heel on floor, toes up on wall. Keep knee straight for gastrocnemius, slight bend for soleus. Feel the difference.
exercises.why: Post-run essential. Prevents plantar fasciitis and Achilles issues as run volume builds.

templates.progression_notes: If the left hip iliac crest is flaring, do an extra set of pigeon pose on the left and add 90-90 hip stretch. If post-badminton, prioritize thoracic rotation and the glute roll.

templates.title: Strength A
templates.subtitle: Lower body + core
templates.coaching_note: Hip hinge on RDLs — push hips back, not down. If lower back flares on RDLs, swap to bird dogs. Shoulder & Elbow phase is optional if you're cooked — but do the elbow work if at all possible.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge
exercises.form_cue: Drive through heels, squeeze glutes at top. Keep ribs down.
exercises.why: Activates glutes before loading them. Protects lower back.

exercises.name: Leg Swings (front-back)
exercises.form_cue: Hold wall for balance. Controlled swing, not a flail.
exercises.why: Loosens hip flexors and hamstrings before squats and RDLs.

exercises.name: Hip Circles
exercises.form_cue: Big slow circles. Feel the hip socket open up.
exercises.why: Hip mobility for squats and split squats.

phases.name: Lower Body
phases.coaching_note: Start at 8kg per side. Add 1kg when all reps feel controlled with good form.

exercises.name: Goblet Squat
exercises.form_cue: Hold one DB at chest. Chest tall, knees track toes, sit into heels. Don't let lower back round at the bottom.
exercises.why: Builds quad and glute strength. Goblet position keeps torso upright — easier on lower back than back squat.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift (DB)
exercises.form_cue: Push hips back, not down. Soft knee bend. DBs stay close to legs. Feel the hamstring stretch. Stop when lower back starts to round — that's your end range.
exercises.why: Hamstring and posterior chain strength. Critical for running economy and knee health.

exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.form_cue: Back foot on dip bar or chair. Front foot far enough forward so knee doesn't cave over toes. Torso upright. Add DBs when bodyweight feels easy.
exercises.why: Single-leg strength to address right-side dominance and imbalance. Protects knees on long runs.

exercises.name: Ankle Weight Leg Raises
exercises.form_cue: Lying on back. Lower back stays flat on floor throughout. Slow and controlled — no swinging.
exercises.why: Hip flexor and lower abs strength. Helps running posture and protects the lower back.

phases.name: Core
phases.coaching_note: These are your lower back's insurance policy. Don't rush them.

exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.form_cue: Lower back pressed firmly into floor the whole time. Opposite arm and leg extend slow. If lower back lifts, shorten the range.
exercises.why: Deep core stability without spinal loading. Safe for the lower back flag.

exercises.name: Bird Dog
exercises.form_cue: On all fours. Don't let the hip drop or rotate. Extend opposite arm and leg until parallel to floor. Hold 2 seconds.
exercises.why: QL and erector stabilisation. Sub for RDLs if lower back flared during the session.

exercises.name: Plank
exercises.form_cue: Forearms down. Body straight line — no sagging hips, no raised butt. Breathe.
exercises.why: Full anterior core endurance. Foundation for everything else.

exercises.name: Side Plank
exercises.form_cue: Hip stacked, don't let it sag. Modified on knee if needed.
exercises.why: Lateral core strength — directly targets the left QL and iliac crest area.

phases.name: Shoulder & Elbow
phases.coaching_note: Skip the shoulder work if you're cooked. But do the elbow exercises even when tired — that eccentric loading is the healing mechanism.

exercises.name: External Rotation (band)
exercises.form_cue: Anchor band at waist height. Elbow bent 90 degrees, pinned to side. Rotate forearm outward only — elbow doesn't move. Slow and controlled.
exercises.why: Rotator cuff strengthening — builds the stabilizers that protect the shoulder during badminton overheads.

exercises.name: Y Raise (light DB)
exercises.form_cue: Slight forward lean. Arms form a Y at 45 degrees, thumbs up. Raise to shoulder height only. Use 2-3kg max.
exercises.why: Targets lower trapezius and posterior deltoid — prevents shoulder impingement on overhead movements.

exercises.name: Wrist Flexor Stretch
exercises.form_cue: Arm straight, palm up. Gently pull fingers back with other hand. Feel the stretch on the inside of the forearm. Don't force it.
exercises.why: Lengthens the wrist flexors and medial forearm tendons — addresses the golfer's elbow tension site.

exercises.name: Eccentric Wrist Curl
exercises.form_cue: Forearm on thigh, palm up, 2-3kg DB. Use other hand to lift the weight up. Lower slowly with working arm only — 3 counts down. That slow lowering is the whole point.
exercises.why: Eccentric loading is the gold-standard treatment for golfer's elbow. The slow lowering phase remodels the tendon.

exercises.name: Reverse Wrist Curl
exercises.form_cue: Forearm on thigh, palm down, 2-3kg DB. Curl knuckles up toward you, lower slowly. Don't let the forearm lift off the thigh.
exercises.why: Strengthens the wrist extensors — the antagonist to the injured flexor group. Balanced forearm strength reduces tendon stress.

templates.progression_notes: Week 1-2: 8kg DBs. Add 1kg per side every 1-2 weeks when all reps are clean. If lower back is sore, drop RDLs and add an extra set of bird dogs.

templates.title: Strength B
templates.subtitle: Upper body + core
templates.coaching_note: Monitor elbow throughout — the rehab exercises at the end are the fix, not just caution. Face pulls and band work protect the shoulder long-term.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Apart
exercises.form_cue: Arms straight. Pull band to chest, squeeze shoulder blades together at end. Control the return.
exercises.why: Activates rear delts and rotator cuff. Counteracts the shoulder internal rotation pattern from desk work and badminton.

exercises.name: Shoulder Circles
exercises.form_cue: Big slow circles forward and back. Let the shoulder blade move.
exercises.why: Shoulder and thoracic mobility before pressing.

exercises.name: Cat-Cow
exercises.form_cue: On all fours. Breathe in on cow (belly drops), breathe out on cat (spine rounds). Slow.
exercises.why: Thoracic mobility. Restricted thoracic spine forces the lumbar to compensate — this helps.

phases.name: Upper Body
phases.coaching_note: Monitor elbow throughout. Any sharp pain on these movements — stop, drop weight, reassess.

exercises.name: DB Floor Press
exercises.form_cue: Lying on floor, arms 45 degrees from body. Press up, don't let elbows flare wide. The floor limits depth — protects the shoulder at the bottom.
exercises.why: Chest and tricep strength without the shoulder strain of a bench. Floor limits depth, making it elbow-friendlier.

exercises.name: DB Bent-Over Row
exercises.form_cue: Hinge at hips, back flat. Pull DB to hip — not to chest. Elbow stays close to body. Squeeze the lat at the top.
exercises.why: Back and lat strength. Pulling strength balances all the pushing. Critical for posture.

exercises.name: Face Pull (resistance tube)
exercises.form_cue: Anchor tube at face height. Pull handles to ears, elbows high. Externally rotate at the end — thumbs back.
exercises.why: Rear delt and rotator cuff strength. Directly counters badminton's internal rotation load. Completely safe on the elbow.

exercises.name: Lateral Raise (pilates band)
exercises.form_cue: Stand on band. Arms slightly in front of body, slight bend in elbow. Raise to shoulder height only. Don't shrug.
exercises.why: Side delt strength for shoulder stability. Using the light pilates band keeps load off the elbow.

exercises.name: Push-Up
exercises.form_cue: Hands just outside shoulders. Body straight line. Lower chest to floor, press all the way up. Stop 2 reps before failure — don't grind.
exercises.why: Bodyweight pressing strength. AMRAP means go until form breaks, not until you collapse.

exercises.name: Y Raise (light DB)
exercises.form_cue: Slight forward lean. Arms form a Y at 45 degrees, thumbs up. Raise to shoulder height only. Use 2-3kg max.
exercises.why: Targets lower trapezius and posterior deltoid — prevents shoulder impingement on overhead movements.

exercises.name: External Rotation (band)
exercises.form_cue: Anchor band at waist height. Elbow bent 90 degrees, pinned to side. Rotate forearm outward only. Slow and controlled.
exercises.why: Rotator cuff strengthening — builds the stabilizers that protect the shoulder during badminton overheads.

exercises.name: Hollow Hold
exercises.form_cue: On back. Arms overhead, legs raised. Lower back pressed to floor. If lower back arches, tuck knees in.
exercises.why: Full anterior chain tension. Carries over to running posture.

exercises.name: Side Plank
exercises.form_cue: Hip stacked. Don't let it sag. Breathe normally.
exercises.why: Lateral core — protecting that left iliac crest.

exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.form_cue: Lower back stays flat. Extend opposite arm and leg slowly. Return slowly.
exercises.why: Deep core stability to end the session.

phases.name: Elbow Rehab
phases.coaching_note: Even when tired, do the eccentric curls. That slow lowering is what heals the tendon.

exercises.name: Wrist Flexor Stretch
exercises.form_cue: Arm straight, palm up. Gently pull fingers back with other hand. Feel the stretch on the inside of the forearm. Don't force it.
exercises.why: Lengthens the wrist flexors and medial forearm tendons — addresses the golfer's elbow tension site.

exercises.name: Eccentric Wrist Curl
exercises.form_cue: Forearm on thigh, palm up, 2-3kg DB. Use other hand to lift the weight up. Lower slowly with working arm only — 3 counts down. That slow lowering is the whole point.
exercises.why: Eccentric loading is the gold-standard treatment for golfer's elbow. The slow lowering phase remodels the tendon.

exercises.name: Reverse Wrist Curl
exercises.form_cue: Forearm on thigh, palm down, 2-3kg DB. Curl knuckles up toward you, lower slowly. Don't let the forearm lift off the thigh.
exercises.why: Strengthens the wrist extensors — the antagonist to the injured flexor group. Balanced forearm strength reduces tendon stress.

templates.progression_notes: Push-ups: when you can consistently hit 15+ reps per set, add a resistance band across your back for extra load. DB exercises: add 1kg per side every 1-2 weeks when all reps are clean.
